summary: Self-improving ML experiment loop: agent modifies code, trains 5 min, keeps improvements, discards failures, repeats autonomously. 66k+ stars, 700 experiments in 2 days, 20 optimizations discovered without human input.
relevanceToAgentForge: Medium — the keep-what-works/discard-failures loop pattern is inspirational but its direct adaptation (prompt template evolution) is already covered by HID-70 and HID-87.

source: EAGER: Efficient Failure Management for Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Reasoning-trace contrastive learning for multi-agent failure detection and reflexive mitigation. Step-wise detection identifies failures at each stage; triggers self-reflect, replan, or regenerate. Continuously improves failure knowledge base.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly addresses AIBlocked recovery, but the core pattern (structured failure taxonomy + retry) is largely covered by HID-78, HID-86, HID-57, HID-90. The contrastive embedding angle is novel but requires ML infrastructure AgentForge doesn't have yet.

source: PROBE: Debugging the Debuggers — Failure-Anchored Structured Recovery
summary: Categorises coding agent failure modes at the step level; transforms failed execution traces into targeted recovery guidance. Identifies recurring misbehaviors and provides structured remediation steps.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — maps to plan repair and AIBlocked recovery, but HID-78 (structured failure taxonomy), HID-81 (reflection-driven plan repair), and HID-86 (AIBlocked recovery with failure memory) already cover this space substantially.

summary: Predictor model dynamically infers step values from current state while Approximation and Target agents execute in parallel. Verification proceeds in parallel threads, reducing end-to-end latency significantly vs autoregressive planning.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — speculative parallel execution requires running multiple subprocess agents concurrently, which breaks AgentForge's deterministic sequential state machine. Feasibility is poor without major architectural changes.

source: Speculative Actions: A Lossless Framework for Faster Agentic Systems (arxiv:2510.04371)
summary: Uses faster models to predict likely future actions and execute them in parallel, committing only when predictions match. Inspired by speculative decoding. Reduces agentic system latency significantly.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — same feasibility concern as 2509.01920; requires parallel subprocess execution not supported by current ProcessRunner architecture.

summary: 7B RL-trained orchestrator routes tasks among GPT-5, Claude Sonnet 4, Gemini 2.5 Pro. Trained with RL to design communication topologies and prompt-engineer focused instructions for each worker. Achieves 83.9% LiveCodeBench, 87.5% GPQA-Diamond.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly addresses static agent-to-stage mapping, but HID-91 (confidence-based dynamic model routing) and HID-85 (complexity-aware model cascade) already cover the routing space. RL training infrastructure is out of scope for 1-2 sprint issues.

source: Dynamic Model Routing and Cascading Survey (arxiv:2603.04445)
summary: Comprehensive 2026 survey of complexity-based LLM routing for efficient inference. Covers ModernBERT-based classifiers, RL routers (xRouter, PROTEUS), confidence-aware frameworks. 37% of enterprises now use 5+ models in production.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — strong evidence base for model routing, but the practical routing enhancements (HID-77, HID-80, HID-85, HID-91) are already covered by existing issues.

summary: Decouples agentic rollout orchestration from RL training loop via 3-stage assembly line (INIT/RUN/EVAL workers). Nearly doubles Qwen3-8B SWE-Bench Verified performance (9.6%→18.0%). Open-sourced in NVIDIA NeMo Gym.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — targets RL training infrastructure. AgentForge uses fixed CLI agents, not RL-trained models. The assembly-line pattern has indirect inspiration value but is out of scope for 1-2 sprints.

summary: Multi-agent framework for episodic context reconstruction in LLM agents. Manager stores compressed historical trajectories as structured workflow summaries; retrieves using semantic similarity, quality rewards, and frequency signals.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — episodic memory is relevant, but the cross-run memory space is extensively covered already: HID-17, HID-27, HID-53, HID-84, HID-87, HID-92.

summary: Instruments code with diagnostic probes, conducts causal analysis on runtime traces, and uses Historical Lesson Learning Mechanism (HLLM) to distill insights from prior failed repair attempts. 34.43% relative improvement in Pass@1.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — historical lesson learning aligns with cross-run memory needs, but this space is already covered by HID-84 and HID-92. The trace instrumentation angle is novel but out of scope.

source: Staircase Streaming for Low-Latency Multi-Agent Inference (arxiv:2510.05059)
summary: Begins generating final response from partial outputs of intermediate pipeline steps, reducing time-to-first-token by up to 93% in multi-agent pipelines while maintaining response quality.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — requires streaming partial outputs between stages; current ProcessRunner collects full output before advancing. HID-48 already covers incremental SSE streaming for UI progress. Architectural alignment is poor.

source: XAI for Coding Agent Failures: Transforming Raw Execution Traces (arxiv:2603.05941)
summary: Systematic XAI approach transforming raw agent execution traces into structured human-interpretable explanations. Users understand failures 2.8× faster than raw traces; 82% automated failure categorisation accuracy.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly inspired HID-94 (run narrative synthesis at ReadyForHumanReview). Validates that structured trace summaries significantly improve human reviewer efficiency.

summary: Multiple agents reflect on shared failures from structurally different perspectives, integrating diverse critic personas into episodic memory. Consistently outperforms single-reviewer baselines on HotPotQA and HumanEval-Python.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — adversarial critique from a distinct perspective is the key insight that inspired HID-93. The multi-perspective reflection addresses blind spots that constructive review misses.

source: Reflection-Driven Control for Trustworthy Code Agents (arxiv:2512.21354)
summary: Formalises how adversarial critique passes catch 23% more implementation-blocking plan defects than constructive review alone. Dedicated adversarial critics are explicitly prompted to find what constructive reviewers miss.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — key empirical evidence supporting HID-93. Quantifies the improvement from adding an adversarial critique stage before plan approval.

summary: LangGraph v1.2 ships built-in state compaction that condenses historical node state before each invocation. Reduces token consumption 40-60% in long-running multi-step agent pipelines without quality loss.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — directly inspired HID-95 (inter-stage context compression). Validates that pre-stage context condensation is production-proven and implementable within state machine architectures.

summary: Anthropic ships context compaction for long Claude sessions (40-60% token reduction) and Outcomes feature: rubric-driven evaluation summaries attached to completed agent runs. Commercial validation of both compression and narrative synthesis patterns.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — dual validation source for HID-95 (context compaction) and HID-94 (run narrative via Outcomes pattern). Both features are now Anthropic-endorsed patterns for production agent systems.

summary: Extension of Karpathy's AutoResearch that applies the self-improvement loop to the research loop itself — a meta-agent optimising the experimental strategy, not just the model.
relevanceToAgentForge: Low — the meta-optimization of AgentForge's own orchestration configuration is an interesting long-term direction but too early for 1-2 sprint implementation. Requires significant historical execution data.

source: Trace2Skill: Distill Trajectory-Local Lessons into Transferable Agent Skills (arxiv:2603.25158)
summary: Mines execution traces for pass and failure modes, summarises actionable lessons, uses evolutionary mutation and selection to produce task-specific reusable skills.
relevanceToAgentForge: High — relevant to cross-run skill learning, but this space is already comprehensively covered by HID-87 (scheduled cross-run skill consolidation) and HID-92 (heuristic memory via ERL).
